title: Multi-mesh decomposition + 9-pivot joint hierarchy for limb animation
intent: S101 ships the procbomber generator as ONE merged BufferGeometry. That's a fine v0 for previewing static silhouettes, but it BLOCKS real limb animation: there is no way for an animation system to rotate the right arm independently of the torso when both arms + torso live as merged vertices inside a single BufferGeometry. Before S102's animation pack lands, the generator must decompose into a node tree of separate meshes (one per actuated body part) parented by pivot Object3Ds. The pivot hierarchy is fixed at 9 actuated joints + 6 visible mesh parts. Each pivot is a Three.js Object3D with no geometry — animation rotates the pivot, the child mesh inherits. This is the design intent (no Mixamo, no SkinnedMesh, no weight paint per the project-procedural-characters memory); it's also the only way to keep the animation systems in GDP-2026-05-20-009 trivially testable.
rationale: Source: `docs/game-design/characters-and-visual.md` §2.2 (original node-tree design) + S101 sprint notes ('the full S102 animation systems will replace them with proper limb-anchor animation' — that landing requires this decomposition). Memory: project-procedural-characters (no skinned mesh, no Mixamo). The current merged-geometry shape was a reasonable v0 — bench renders are cheap and reroll is fast — but it cannot host per-limb rotation. Three actuated joints per arm (shoulder + elbow + wrist) would be a full humanoid rig; two (shoulder + elbow) is enough for the toy-scale walk cycle + bomb-place reach we actually need. Same for legs (hip + knee, no ankle). Plus one neck pivot for slapstick head shake. Total: 9 actuated pivots.
acceptanceHints: examples/procbomber-bench/src/generators/bomber-mesh.ts: generateBomberMesh() is REPLACED (or wrapped) by generateBomberMeshTree() which returns { root: Object3D, parts: BomberMeshTreeNodes } instead of a single BufferGeometry. The single-geometry export stays as a deprecated path (or as a baked variant) for the existing tests; the bench bootstrap switches to the tree path.
Node tree structure (fixed shape — animation systems and ECS components depend on these exact names):
root (Object3D, no mesh) — entity Transform anchors here, follows GridPosition
└── torso (Mesh, capsule-or-box geometry, palette.torso color)
├── neck (Object3D pivot, no mesh, attached at top of torso)
│   └── head (Mesh, sphere/box geometry, palette.head color, +secondary stripe via vertex color)
├── shoulder.l (Object3D pivot, no mesh, attached at upper-left of torso)
│   └── upperArm.l (Mesh, cylinder/box geometry, palette.limbs color)
│       └── elbow.l (Object3D pivot, no mesh, attached at end of upperArm)
│           └── forearm.l (Mesh, cylinder/box geometry, palette.limbs color, includes small hand at end)
├── shoulder.r / upperArm.r / elbow.r / forearm.r (mirror)
├── hip.l (Object3D pivot, no mesh, attached at bottom-left of torso)
│   └── upperLeg.l (Mesh, cylinder/box geometry, palette.limbs color)
│       └── knee.l (Object3D pivot, no mesh, attached at end of upperLeg)
│           └── lowerLeg.l (Mesh, cylinder/box geometry, palette.limbs color, includes small foot at end)
└── hip.r / upperLeg.r / knee.r / lowerLeg.r (mirror)

Actuated pivot count: 9 = 1 neck + 2 shoulders + 2 elbows + 2 hips + 2 knees. Visible mesh count: 6 (torso + head + 2 upperArm + 2 upperLeg) + 4 (2 forearm with hand, 2 lowerLeg with foot) = 10 meshes (the forearm/lowerLeg merge their hand/foot into the same BufferGeometry to keep draw-call count down).

Each pivot Object3D's local position equals the anchor where it attaches to its parent mesh. Rotation defaults to identity (T-pose-ish: arms hang down, legs straight). Animation systems mutate ONLY the pivot's `rotation`, never its `position` (positions are determined by the parent mesh's geometry).
Naming convention is non-negotiable: animation systems will grep for these exact names. Lowercase, dot-suffix for laterality, no underscore variants.

ECS surface: a new `LimbPivots` component (under examples/procbomber-bench/schemas/scene-extensions.schema.json, then promoted to examples/kaboom-crew/ when GDP-2026-05-21-007 lands) carries entity-side references to each pivot Object3D so animation systems can look them up without traversing the tree every tick. Shape: `LimbPivots { neck, shoulderL, shoulderR, elbowL, elbowR, hipL, hipR, kneeL, kneeR }` — nine fields, each a renderer-handle id.

Draw-call budget: 10 meshes per bomber × up to 4 bombers = 40 draw calls before instancing. Acceptable per `docs/game-design/visual-style.md` §11 budget (< 200 / frame). If the test bot-vs-bot scene shows perf regression, the forearm+hand and lowerLeg+foot merges can be split further only if doctor flags an instancing opportunity.

Backwards compatibility: the existing `geometry.userData.anchors` BomberAnchors stays — generateBomberMeshTree() exports anchor world positions on the root userData so QA + agent probes can still query 'where is the head' without walking the tree.

Unit tests: tree-shape invariants (always 9 pivots + 10 meshes), naming invariants (every pivot/mesh has the expected name), pivot positions match the size knobs (shoulder.l.x equals -halfTorso - armWidth/2, etc), default pose is T-pose-ish (all rotations identity).

Bench integration: bench-state's buildBomberGeometry call site becomes buildBomberMeshTree, and adapter.setMeshGeometry becomes a recursive 'swap the geometries on each named mesh node' operation. The bench's rebuild loop on slider drag keeps working — just touches more nodes per rebuild.

Existing S101 stub animations (idle-bob, walk-swing) keep working but acquire a new mode: walk-swing in the multi-mesh world rotates shoulder.l/r and hip.l/r in counter-phase instead of moving the whole entity in X. Idle-bob still drives root.position.y. The bench dropdown gets a third stub: 'limb-test' which rotates each pivot by 0.3 rad one at a time to visually verify the hierarchy.

BLOCKING for: GDP-2026-05-20-009 (full animation pack — six systems) — that story's hit-recoil / walk-cycle / bomb-place-reach / death-spring all need named pivots to act on. Do NOT plan S102 animation work before this lands.
NOT in scope here: ankle / wrist pivots (deferred — toy-scale doesn't need them), spine-split (single torso is fine for MVP), finger curls (no fingers in the mesh), facial nodes (no face).
Document the joint-hierarchy figure in `docs/game-design/characters-and-visual.md` §2.2 — replace the ASCII tree with the post-decomposition one when this story merges.
